one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-and-framework survey that recasts LLM alignment history as the evolution of reward design (rule-based -> data-driven -> hybrid; RL-based -> RL-free), useful as a map of reward-hacking/misspecification mitigations but empirical of nothing itself.
key_finding: The organizing thesis: the trajectory of LLM alignment is best read as a continuous refinement of reward design, evolving along two axes -- (a) from rule specification to preference learning to behaviorally-grounded reward inference (e.g., inverse RL) in how RMs are constructed, and (b) from RL-based explicit RMs (RLHF/PPO) toward RL-free / implicit-reward paradigms (DPO and SFT-style objectives) in how rewards are optimized. The survey argues reward design is the 'linchpin' that determines downstream behavior, but concludes with the caveat that explicit reward modeling is NOT indispensable: reward-free methods (DPO, SFT variants) that fold the reward directly into the optimization objective are an increasingly central research trend. Hybrid reward mechanisms (fusing rule-based + data-driven + verifiable signals via weighted, sequential, modular/hierarchical, or attention/gating fusion) are highlighted as the most robust route for multi-objective, noisy, real-world alignment.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical study) that reframes the whole history of LLM alignment as the progressive refinement of REWARD DESIGN, and organizes the reward-modeling literature into a single hierarchical taxonomy. The authors' own contributions are three: (1) a macro-level taxonomy of reward models (RMs) along complementary axes -- numerical vs. non-numerical, rule-based vs. data-driven vs. hybrid, explicit vs. implicit, plus finer splits (pointwise/preferencewise, pairwise/listwise, response-level/token-level, single-value/multi-value); (2) a 'diagnosis-prescription-treatment' medical-metaphor conceptual framework that casts feedback as diagnosis, reward design as prescription, and optimization as treatment, with the LLM as the patient and HHH (helpful/harmless/honest) as the clinical target; (3) a synthesis of paradigm shifts -- from RL-based to RL-free optimization and from single-task to multi-objective/multi-modal settings -- and a dedicated consolidation of HYBRID reward design (multi-source, multi-aspect, multi-granularity, multi-stage, multi-modal) into a coherent design space. All specific methods named (DPO, GRPO, PPO variants, TLCR, TDPO, RBR, AutoRule, ArmoRM, Rewarded Soup, etc.) are CITED prior work being organized, not the paper's own systems.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model; this is a survey and does not propose or evaluate an attack or defense. The security-relevant failure modes it organizes the literature around are internal to the alignment pipeline: reward hacking (models exploiting flaws in the reward function to inflate scores without genuine alignment), reward misspecification (gap between the designed reward signal and users' latent preferences), reward-model overfitting to annotator bias, mode collapse from over-optimization on narrow reward signals, and distributional-shift / generalization failures of learned RMs. It frames well-structured reward design (e.g., rule-based safety constraints, factually-augmented RMs, multi-source verifiable signals) as the mitigation lever for these, tied to safety/harmlessness, factuality, privacy, and controllability objectives.
limitations: As a survey it runs no experiments and reports no measured results, so all comparative claims are qualitative and inherited from cited papers. The taxonomy's axes overlap and are not mutually exclusive (a method can be hybrid, multi-value, and implicit at once), and the medical 'diagnosis-prescription-treatment' metaphor is illustrative rather than a predictive or falsifiable framework. Coverage is a curated snapshot of fast-moving 2023-2025 work and is inevitably incomplete. The survey does not quantify how much reward hacking or misspecification each mitigation actually reduces -- it cites that methods like AutoRule or Factually-Augmented RLHF 'reduced reward hacking' without independent measurement.
